prompt:
In a review of a biography, a critic says the author’s prose is “terse”: even major turning points are recounted in clipped sentences with little extra detail.
As used in the text, what does “terse” most nearly mean?

choices.A: brief and abrupt
choices.B: cheerful
choices.C: richly descriptive
choices.D: hesitant

explanation: The passage says the prose is clipped and includes little extra detail, so “terse” means brief and abrupt.

prompt:
Some apartment buildings have installed angled ledges above south-facing windows. The ledges block high summer sun but still allow lower winter sunlight to enter. In some buildings, that reduces overheating during hot months without darkening rooms throughout the year.
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: Angled window ledges can reduce summer overheating while still allowing winter sunlight
choices.B: South-facing windows should always be covered during every season
choices.C: Indoor overheating can be solved only by replacing all apartment windows
choices.D: Winter sunlight is more harmful to apartments than summer sunlight

explanation: The text emphasizes blocking high summer sun while still admitting lower winter light.

prompt:
Before bakeries adopted bread trays with common dimensions, neighborhood grocers often had to rearrange deliveries by hand because trays from one bakery would not stack securely with trays from another. Once standard tray sizes spread, stores could move and store bread deliveries more quickly.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Standard tray sizes improved efficiency in storing and moving bread deliveries
choices.B: Grocers preferred to unload bread by hand even after standard trays became common
choices.C: Bakeries adopted standard trays mainly to reduce the weight of each loaf
choices.D: Standard trays eliminated the need for bread to be delivered daily

explanation: The passage explains that standard trays stacked securely and let stores move deliveries more quickly.

prompt:
An archaeologist notices that the stone at a doorway threshold is worn smooth only in a narrow strip at its center. She points to that pattern as evidence that people passed repeatedly through the doorway over many years.
Which choice best describes the function of the first sentence in the text?

choices.A: It presents physical evidence that the second sentence interprets
choices.B: It introduces a theory that the second sentence rejects
choices.C: It explains why the doorway was difficult to preserve
choices.D: It compares two buildings that the second sentence later contrasts

explanation: The smooth wear pattern is physical evidence used to infer long-term foot traffic.

prompt:
A student is writing about shared tool sheds at community gardens and has taken these notes:
• Some community gardens keep basic tools such as trowels and rakes in a locked shed
• New gardeners can borrow the tools instead of buying their own right away
• Volunteers track returns and do simple maintenance
• The program can lower the cost of trying gardening
The student wants to emphasize how the tool shed lowers the barrier for beginners. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Some community gardens keep basic tools such as trowels and rakes in a locked shed
choices.B: Volunteers track returns and do simple maintenance
choices.C: By lending basic tools, community gardens can make gardening less expensive for beginners to try
choices.D: New gardeners often use trowels and rakes

explanation: This choice directly captures the idea that borrowing tools lowers the cost of trying gardening.

prompt:
The town installed a digital queue screen at the permit office. ______, visitors no longer crowd around a single service window to ask whose turn is next.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: Nevertheless,
choices.B: As a result,
choices.C: For instance,
choices.D: Similarly,

explanation: The second sentence describes the effect of installing the digital queue screen.

prompt:
The museum’s smallest clock, <u>restored by apprentices in 1998,</u> is displayed near the entrance.
Which choice best punctuates the underlined portion of the text?

choices.A: NO CHANGE
choices.B: ; restored by apprentices in 1998;
choices.C: : restored by apprentices in 1998:
choices.D: —restored by apprentices in 1998—

explanation: The phrase adds nonessential information and is correctly set off with commas.

prompt:
The profile praises the chef’s inventive menus, <u>it says almost nothing about the dishwashers and servers who kept the restaurant running.</u>
Which choice most effectively revises the underlined portion of the text to maintain the grammatical correctness of the sentence?

choices.A: it says almost nothing about the dishwashers and servers who kept the restaurant running
choices.B: and it says almost nothing about the dishwashers and servers who kept the restaurant running
choices.C: but it says almost nothing about the dishwashers and servers who kept the restaurant running
choices.D: saying almost nothing about the dishwashers and servers who kept the restaurant running

explanation: Two independent clauses need a conjunction, and “but” correctly signals contrast.

prompt:
The final seating chart was sent only to Marco and <u>I</u> before rehearsal.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: I
choices.B: me
choices.C: my
choices.D: myself

explanation: The pronoun is the object of the preposition “to,” so “me” is correct.

prompt:
Since 2016, the community theater <u>hosts</u> free outdoor preview nights each summer.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: hosts
choices.B: hosted
choices.C: has hosted
choices.D: will host

explanation: “Since 2016” calls for the present perfect tense.

prompt:
After checking the spreadsheet one last time, <u>the duplicated entry was easy for Tessa to notice.</u>
Which choice best revises the text to ensure that the modifying phrase is correctly placed?

choices.A: the duplicated entry was easy for Tessa to notice
choices.B: Tessa noticed the duplicated entry
choices.C: the spreadsheet made the duplicated entry easy for Tessa to notice
choices.D: easy for Tessa to notice, the duplicated entry appeared after the check

explanation: The introductory phrase describes Tessa’s action, so Tessa should be the subject of the main clause.

prompt:
[1] Some museums now place folding sketch stools near large paintings. [2] Visitors can sit briefly and draw details they notice in the artwork. [3] Because the stools fold flat, staff can move them easily when galleries become crowded. [4] In some museums, pencils and clipboards are available at a nearby desk.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“The stools can encourage slower looking without requiring a formal class or tour.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The sentence best follows the description of visitors sketching details and explains the benefit before the paragraph shifts to logistics.

prompt:
A teacher recorded the average number of specimen cards four student groups sorted in fifteen minutes.
Group A: 19
Group B: 16
Group C: 21
Group D: 18
Which choice is best supported by the data?

choices.A: Group A sorted more cards, on average, than Group D
choices.B: Group B sorted the most cards, on average
choices.C: Group C sorted the most cards, on average
choices.D: The average number of cards sorted increased steadily from Group A through Group D

explanation: Group C’s 21 cards is the highest value in the table.

prompt:
The reviewer describes the documentary as “evenhanded,” noting that it considers objections as well as supporting evidence.
As used in the text, what does “evenhanded” most nearly mean?

choices.A: balanced and fair
choices.B: fast-paced
choices.C: doubtful
choices.D: personal

explanation: The reviewer says the documentary considers objections as well as supporting evidence, so “evenhanded” means balanced and fair.

prompt:
In a short story, the narrator says, “My mother kept the old ferry token folded inside a teacup by the sink. It bought no passage now, but when she turned it over with her thumb, the kitchen seemed to remember mornings before the bridge.”
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: The token reminds the mother of a lost daily routine
choices.B: The mother plans to use the token once the ferry reopens
choices.C: The kitchen matters only because the token is stored there
choices.D: The token is kept because it is made of valuable metal

explanation: The token no longer functions practically, but it recalls ferry commutes from the past.

prompt:
In a greenhouse trial, basil plants grown beside reflective white stakes produced straighter stems than plants grown beside dark stakes. The researchers suggest that the lighter stakes scattered more light onto shaded sides of the plants.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Reflective white stakes may promote straighter basil growth by increasing side light
choices.B: Basil plants can grow properly only beside white supports
choices.C: Dark stakes always prevent basil from receiving enough light
choices.D: The trial proved that support color matters more than watering in every case

explanation: The passage connects straighter growth with the lighter stakes’ ability to scatter more light to shaded sides.

prompt:
A sociologist writes, “The tea stall was the neighborhood’s unofficial newsroom.”
Why does the author most likely include the quotation in the text?

choices.A: To present one sociologist’s view of the tea stall as a place where local information circulated
choices.B: To prove that all neighborhood news came from tea sellers
choices.C: To explain how tea was prepared at roadside stalls
choices.D: To describe the physical layout of one particular stall

explanation: The quotation presents the tea stall as a local information hub.

prompt:
A student is writing about public pianos in train stations and has taken these notes:
• Some stations keep pianos in waiting areas
• Travelers can play briefly while waiting for a train
• Signs invite respectful, short performances
• The pianos can make waiting spaces feel more welcoming
Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to make a general claim about public pianos?

choices.A: Public pianos can make waiting areas feel more welcoming by giving travelers a chance to play briefly
choices.B: Most train stations should replace all seating with pianos
choices.C: Travelers usually prefer silence to music while waiting for trains
choices.D: Respectful performances are possible only by trained musicians

explanation: This choice combines the notes about brief performances and a more welcoming atmosphere.

prompt:
The article begins with hand-copied recipe notebooks sold at village fairs. ______, it turns to the cheaply printed pamphlets that largely replaced them in the 1890s.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: Likewise,
choices.B: Later,
choices.C: Otherwise,
choices.D: For example,

explanation: The article moves from handwritten notebooks to the printed pamphlets that replaced them later.

prompt:
The internship asks students to sort seed packets, weigh samples, and <u>update donor records.</u>
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: updating donor records
choices.B: update donor records
choices.C: updated donor records
choices.D: to update donor records

explanation: The series requires parallel base-form verbs: sort, weigh, and update.

prompt:
The volunteers <u>who arrived before sunrise</u> finished setting up the booths first.
Which choice best describes the underlined portion of the sentence?

choices.A: It is a nonessential clause that should be set off with commas
choices.B: It is a restrictive clause that correctly identifies which volunteers finished setting up the booths
choices.C: It creates a comma splice
choices.D: It should be moved to the end of the sentence for clarity

explanation: The clause identifies a specific group of volunteers.

prompt:
A basket of pears and plums <u>sits</u> on the counter beside the window.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: sit
choices.B: sits
choices.C: have sat
choices.D: sitting

explanation: The singular subject “basket” takes the singular verb “sits.”

prompt:
A student wrote the following sentence in a draft:
“When the council delayed the vote, this frustrated several residents.”
Which choice best explains why the sentence should be revised?

choices.A: The sentence should be revised because the pronoun “this” has an unclear antecedent
choices.B: The sentence should be revised because councils should not delay votes
choices.C: The sentence should be revised because the verb should be in the future tense
choices.D: The sentence should be revised because residents should not be mentioned in formal writing

explanation: It is unclear whether “this” refers to the delay, the vote, or the whole situation.

prompt:
[1] Some apartment lobbies now include shelves for jigsaw-puzzle swaps. [2] Residents can borrow a puzzle, finish it at home, and return it later. [3] Because the shelves sit near mailboxes, people often pause to browse them during daily routines. [4] In some buildings, residents leave short notes about puzzle difficulty or missing pieces.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“The shelves can turn a quick stop in the lobby into a small exchange of entertainment.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The sentence best follows the description of browsing during daily routines and summarizes the shelves’ social function.

prompt:
To make the sentence more concise, the editor replaced “on a weekly basis” with <u>weekly</u>.
Which choice best describes the effect of the revision?

choices.A: It changes the meaning by making the timing less exact
choices.B: It shortens the sentence without changing its meaning
choices.C: It makes the sentence less grammatical
choices.D: It introduces a more formal tone

explanation: “Weekly” is a more concise equivalent of “on a weekly basis.”

prompt:
In an experiment, museum visitors identifying pottery vessels made fewer mistakes when display labels included profile silhouettes in addition to overhead photographs than when the labels included overhead photographs alone.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Additional profile silhouettes may help visitors identify pottery vessels more accurately
choices.B: Overhead photographs are unnecessary in all pottery displays
choices.C: The visitors had memorized all the pottery types before the experiment
choices.D: Vessel profiles are more important than every other visual feature in all cases

explanation: The passage reports fewer errors when profile silhouettes were added.

prompt:
An article about restored grain elevators explains that buildings once used to store harvested wheat are now often repaired and opened to visitors. The article ends by noting that some schools use the elevators during field trips on farming, trade, and rail transport.
Which choice best describes the function of the final sentence in the text?

choices.A: It provides an example of a present-day educational use connected to the restored buildings
choices.B: It argues that all schools should study rail transport through field trips
choices.C: It shifts the discussion away from restoration to unrelated tourism data
choices.D: It denies that repaired grain elevators have any public value

explanation:
The final sentence gives a school field-trip use for the restored grain elevators.
PART D — MODULE 2 ANSWER KEY + EXPLANATIONS
